revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Ippen (Buddhist)]][[en:Ippen]][[fa:ایپن شونین]][[fr:Ippen]][[ja:一遍]][[no:Ippen]][[pl:Ippen]][[sl:Ippen Shonin]][[vi:Nhất Biến]][[zh:一遍]] {{short description|event usually held at shrines and temples in Japan on the Day of the Rooster in November every year}} {{Infobox person|image=File:Ippen2.jpg|birth_date={{birth date|1239|3|21}}|death_date={{death date|1289|9|9}}|birth_place={{ill|Hōgon-ji|qid=Q3146357|1=Hōgon-ji (Matsuyama)}}|death_place={{ill|Shinkoji|qid=Q11582869|1=Shinko-ji Temple}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|qid=Q17|1=Japan}}|occupation={{ill|bhikkhu|qid=Q854997|1=Bhikkhu}}|title={{ill|Yugyō Shōnin|qid=Q28692848|1=Yugyō Shōnin}}|father={{ill|Kawano Michihiro|qid=Q17219045|1=Kawano Michihiro}}|family={{ill|Kōno clan|qid=Q11554232|1=Kōno clan}}|religion={{ill|Ji-shū|qid=Q696966|1=Ji-shu}}; {{ill|Jōdo-shū|qid=Q912208|1=Jōdo-shū}}|native_name=一遍|resting_place={{ill|Shinkoji|qid=Q11582869|1=Shinko-ji Temple}}}}
{{more citations needed|date=March 2014}} [[Image:Ippen2.jpg|right|thumb|250px|Statue of Ippen at {{ill|Shōjōkō-ji|en|Shōjōkō-ji|qid=Q11561284|lt=Shōjōkō-ji|1=Shōjōkō-ji}}, {{ill|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|en|Fujisawa, Kanagawa|qid=Q386661|lt=Fujisawa|1=Fujisawa, Kanagawa}}, Japan]]{{buddhism}} {{nihongo|'''Ippen Shōnin'''|一遍上人|1234–1289|also known as '''Zuien'''}} was a {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|qid=Q736311|lt=Japanese Buddhist|1=Buddhism in Japan}} itinerant preacher (''hijiri'') who founded the {{nihongo|{{ill|Ji-shu|en|Ji-shu|qid=Q696966|lt=Ji-shū|1=Ji-shu}}|時宗||"Time sect"}} branch of {{ill|Pure Land Buddhism|en|Pure Land Buddhism|qid=Q262244|lt=Pure Land Buddhism|1=Pure Land Buddhism}}.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Buswell|editor1-first=Robert Jr|editor2-last=Lopez|editor2-first=Donald S. Jr.|editor1-link=Robert Buswell Jr.|editor2-link=Donald S. Lopez Jr.|title=Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism.|date=2013|publisher={{ill|Princeton University Press|en|Princeton University Press|qid=Q1708210|lt=Princeton University Press|1=Princeton University Press}}|location=Princeton, NJ|page=374|isbn=9780691157863}}</ref>
==Life== Ippen was born at {{ill|Hōgon-ji (Matsuyama)|en|Hōgon-ji (Matsuyama)|qid=Q3146357|lt=Hōgon-ji|1=Hōgon-ji (Matsuyama)}}, a temple in {{ill|Iyo Province|en|Iyo Province|qid=Q907829|lt=Iyo Province|1=Iyo Province}} (modern {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|en|Ehime Prefecture|qid=Q123376|lt=Ehime Prefecture|1=Ehime Prefecture}}) on the island of [[Shikoku]]. He was originally named {{nihongo|Chishin|智真}}. He first studied {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} at {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|qid=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei|1=Mount Hiei}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, and then [[Jōdo-shū]] at {{ill|Dazaifu, Fukuoka|en|Dazaifu, Fukuoka|qid=Q728218|lt=Dazaifu, Fukuoka|1=Dazaifu, Fukuoka}} on [[Kyushu]].<ref name="japanesearchitectureandartnetuserssystem">{{cite web|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/i/ippen.htm|title=JAANUS / Ippen 一遍|author=Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System|work=aisf.or.jp}}</ref>
When his father died, the 25-year old Ippen returned to secular life and assumed family responsibilities. He got married and became head of the household.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Japanese Religions|last=Yusa|first=Michiko|publisher=Routledge|year=2002|isbn=0-415-26284-4|pages=59}}</ref>
During a pilgrimage to the {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|en|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|qid=Q611345|lt=Kumano Shrines|1=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range}}, the [[kami]] enshrined there revealed to Ippen that enlightenment was determined by [[Amitābha]] and that Ippen should devote himself to preaching the importance of reciting his name, a practice called {{ill|Nianfo|en|Nianfo|qid=Q1315754|lt=nembutsu|1=Nianfo}}. Ippen and a band of followers then travelled throughout the country proselytizing with their ecstatic ''nembutsu'' dance,<ref>Moriarty, Elisabeth (1976). [https://web.archive.org/web/20140304073315/http://nirc.nanzan-u.ac.jp/nfile/1035 Nembutsu Odori], Asian Folklore Studies 35 (1), 7-16</ref> and won a wide following among common people.<ref>[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/i/ippen.htm Ippen], Jaanus</ref> Other practices associated with the Ji-shū include scheduled sessions of chanting (hence the name ''Ji-shū'' "Time sect"), the handing out of slips of paper with the ''nembutsu'' written on them,<ref name="Dobbins1988">{{cite journal|last1=Dobbins|first1=James C.|title=Review: No Abode: The Record of Ippen. by Dennis Hirota|journal=Monumenta Nipponica|date=1988|volume=43|issue=2|pages=253|doi=10.2307/2384755|jstor=2384755}}</ref> and keeping a register of the converted.
Ippen's insistence on constant traveling and giving up of family and possessions led to his nicknames: {{nihongo|Traveling Saint|遊行上人|Yugyō Shōnin}} and {{nihongo|Holy Man of Renunciation| 捨聖|Sutehijiri}}.<ref name="japanesearchitectureandartnetuserssystem"/>
== Doctrine == Ippen's doctrine was primarily influenced by {{ill|Shōkū|en|Shōkū|qid=Q7506163|lt=Shōkū|1=Shōkū}}, founder of the {{ill|Seizan|en|Seizan|qid=Q7447036|lt=Seizan|1=Seizan}} branch of the [[Jōdo-shū]], who "insisted that the various Buddhist practices contain no more than a portion of the merit of the single practice of the nembutsu and serve merely to lead people to recite the nembutsu."<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20110610205645/http://www.jsri.jp/English/Pureland/LINEAGE/disciples/ippen.html Ippen (1239-1289), The Ji School, The Votive Nembutsu]</ref> However he was also strongly influenced by the [[:en:nondualism (spirituality)|nondualism]] of {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|qid=Q7953|lt=Zen|1=Zen}} and even received [[Dharma transmission]] as a {{ill|Zen master|en|Zen master|qid=Q1998748|lt=Zen master|1=Zen master}} from {{ill|Rōshi|en|Rōshi|qid=Q1567545|lt=Rōshi|1=Rōshi}} Kakushin.
== Legacy == Before his death Ippen burned all his writings, saying that "they have all become ''namuamidabutsu'' (devotion to Amida Buddha)", but copies were kept by some of his disciples. {{ill|Dennis Hirota|en|Dennis Hirota|qid=Q5258516|lt=Dennis Hirota|1=Dennis Hirota}} has translated some of these writings into English in ''No Abode: The Record of Ippen'' (1997).<ref>Dennis Hirota, 'No Abode: The Record of Ippen'', Ryukoku-Ibs Studies in Buddhist Thought and Tradition, Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawai'i Press|University of Hawai'i Press]] 1997 {{ISBN|0824819977}}</ref>
After Ippen's death many of his disciples appear to have committed suicide, throwing themselves into the sea in the hope of being reborn in the Pure Land. Such phenomena perhaps help to explain the limited spread of the Ji-shu, and the ecstatic fervor of the early Ji-shu also seems to have militated against mainstream acceptance.
In 1292, three years after Ippen's death, Ippen's birthplace, {{ill|Hōgon-ji (Matsuyama)|en|Hōgon-ji (Matsuyama)|qid=Q3146357|lt=Hōgon-ji|1=Hōgon-ji (Matsuyama)}}, was rebuilt by his disciple Sen'a and became a Ji sect temple.
== Art == [[File:Ippen Biography 3.jpg|thumbnail|{{ill|Ippen Shōnin Eden|en|Ippen Shōnin Eden|qid=Q2904124|lt=Illustrated Biography of the Priest Ippen|1=Ippen Shōnin Eden}}: Volume 7. Kamakura period, 1299.]] Ippen himself was greatly devoted to paintings of {{ill|Shandao|en|Shandao|qid=Q1013023|lt=Shandao|1=Shandao}}'s allegory of the "White Path", so it is appropriate that his life led to the production of a great many portraits, sculpted images, and illustrated narrative scrolls (''emaki'' 絵巻).
The ''{{ill|Ippen Shōnin Eden|en|Ippen Shōnin Eden|qid=Q2904124|lt=Ippen Hijiri-e|1=Ippen Shōnin Eden}}'' (一遍聖絵) was edited by Ippen's disciple {{ill|Shōkai|en|Shōkai|qid=Q11611021|lt=Shōkai|1=Shōkai}} (聖戒) and, according to an inscription dated 1299, was painted by the artist [[En'i]] (円伊) ({{ill|Kankikō-ji|en|Kankikō-ji|qid=Q11545313|lt=Kankikō-ji|1=Kankikō-ji}} 歓喜光寺, Kyoto, and Tokyo National Museum). The twelve handscrolls on silk show Ippen's trip around Japan, and are well known for their naturalistic depiction of "famous places", including {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|qid=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}} (富士), Kumano, [[Shitennō-ji]] (四天王寺), Zenkō-ji (善光寺), {{ill|Enoshima|en|Enoshima|qid=Q989803|lt=Enoshima|1=Enoshima}} (江ノ島), Yoshino (吉野), [[Itsukushima]] (厳島), and Naruto (鳴門). The treatment of space shows the influence of {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|qid=Q7462|lt=Song|1=Song dynasty}} and {{ill|Yuan dynasty|en|Yuan dynasty|qid=Q7313|lt=Yuan|1=Yuan dynasty}} Chinese landscape painting. A second type of biographical handscroll ''Ippen Shōnin Engi-e'' 一遍上人縁起絵), edited by Ippen's other disciple, Sōshun (宗俊), was painted sometime between 1304 and 1307. The original scrolls no longer exist but were copied in many other versions including those at Shinkō-ji (真光寺), {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|qid=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. These versions are characterized by the addition of the biography of Ippen's most important disciple, Taa (他阿, 1237–1319). In the Shinkō-ji version, the first four scrolls depict Ippen's life, while the last six concern the life of Taa and the spread of Ji Sect teaching. The Kinren-ji (金蓮寺) in Kyoto has a {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} copy of the now-lost work dated 1307.<ref>[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/i/ippen.htm Ippen]</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Foard, James Harlan (1977). Ippen Shônin and popular Buddhism in Kamakura Japan, Dissertation, Stanford University. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/9782681 OCLC] * Foard, James Harlan(2006). ''The Pure Land Tradition: History and Development,'' Fremont, CA: Jain Publishing. {{ISBN|9780895810922}}. pp. 357–398 * Griffiths, Caitilin J. (2011). [https://tspace.library.utoronto.ca/handle/1807/26185 Tracing the Itinerant Path: Jishū Nuns of Medieval Japan], Thesis, University of Toronto * Hirota, Dennis (1997). ''No Abode: The Record of Ippen'', (Ryukoku-Ibs Studies in Buddhist Thought and Tradition), Honolulu: {{ill|University of Hawaiʻi Press|en|University of Hawaiʻi Press|qid=Q4358493|lt=University of Hawaiʻi Press|1=University of Hawaiʻi Press}}, {{ISBN|0824819977}} * Kaufman, Laura S. (1992). Nature, Courtly Imagery, and Sacred Meaning in the Ippen Hijiri-e. In James H. Sanford (ed.), Flowing Traces Buddhism in the Literary and Visual Arts of Japan, Princeton, N.J.: {{ill|Princeton University Press|en|Princeton University Press|qid=Q1708210|lt=Princeton University Press|1=Princeton University Press}}; pp. 47–75 * Matsunaga, Daigan, Matsunaga, Alicia (1996), Foundation of Japanese buddhism, Vol. 2: The Mass Movement (Kamakura and Muromachi Periods), Los Angeles; Tokyo: Buddhist Books International, 1996. {{ISBN|0-914910-28-0}} * Thornton, S.A. (1999). Charisma and Community Formation in Medieval Japan: The Case of the Yugyo-ha (1300-1700). Cornell East Asia Series no. 102, Ithaca: Cornell University, {{ISBN|1-885445-62-8}}
== Credits == Paragraphs 2,3,4,11,12 are all courtesy of the Japanese Architecture and Art Net Users System.
==External links== {{Wikiquote}} *[https://web.archive.org/web/20050501193416/http://www.jsri.jp/English/Pure%20Land/LINEAGE/disciples.html Jodo Shu Research Institute] *[http://www.nembutsu.info/hsrippen.htm Stewart, Harold, ''Ippen''] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20121103124822/http://www.jideco.co.jp/homes/yugyoji/index.html The Yugyo-ji temple / Head temple of the ji-shu sect] * [http://www.pureland-buddhism.org/WebplsCh/%E6%B7%A8%E5%9C%9F%E5%AE%97%E5%8F%A2%E6%9B%B8/%E5%BF%B5%E4%BD%9B%E9%87%91%E8%A8%80%E9%8C%84/%E7%9B%AE%E9%8C%84.aspx 念佛金言录] * [http://www.tnm.jp/modules/r_collection/index.php?controller=dtl&colid=A10944&lang=en Shonin Ippen, E-den (Biographical stories vol7)] *[https://www.japanese-wiki-corpus.org/Buddhism/The%20Ji%20Sect.html The Ji Sect (時宗)] *[http://www.jishu.or.jp/ Ji-Shu Main Temple website]
